orbit.definition: The curved path an object takes around a star, planet, or moon due to gravity. Described by parameters like eccentricity, inclination, and semi-major axis.

eccentricity.definition: A measure of how much an orbit deviates from a perfect circle. A value of 0 is circular, values approaching 1 are highly elongated ellipses.

semi-major axis.definition: Half the longest diameter of an elliptical orbit. It defines the size of an orbit and is used to calculate orbital period.

inclination.definition: The angle between an orbit's plane and a reference plane (usually the ecliptic for solar system objects). 0 degrees means the orbit lies in the reference plane.

perihelion.definition: The point in an orbit closest to the Sun. Earth reaches perihelion around January 3 each year, at about 147.1 million km.

aphelion.definition: The point in an orbit farthest from the Sun. Earth reaches aphelion around July 4 each year, at about 152.1 million km.

heliosphere.definition: The vast bubble-like region of space dominated by the solar wind, extending far beyond the orbit of Pluto. It shields the solar system from interstellar radiation.

heliopause.definition: The boundary where the solar wind's pressure is balanced by the interstellar medium. It marks the outer edge of the heliosphere, about 123 AU from the Sun.

oort cloud.definition: A theoretical spherical shell of icy objects surrounding the solar system at distances of 2,000 to 100,000 AU. Believed to be the source of long-period comets.

kuiper belt.definition: A region of the solar system beyond Neptune (30-50 AU) containing thousands of icy bodies including dwarf planets like Pluto and Makemake.

asteroid belt.definition: A region between Mars and Jupiter (2.2-3.2 AU) containing millions of rocky bodies. Despite popular imagery, objects are widely spaced.

dwarf planet.definition: A celestial body orbiting the Sun that is massive enough for gravity to make it round, but has not cleared its orbital neighborhood of other debris. Examples: Pluto, Ceres, Eris.

gas giant.definition: A large planet composed primarily of hydrogen and helium, lacking a well-defined solid surface. Jupiter and Saturn are the solar system's gas giants.

ice giant.definition: A giant planet composed mainly of elements heavier than hydrogen and helium, such as water, ammonia, and methane. Uranus and Neptune are ice giants.

terrestrial planet.definition: A rocky planet with a solid surface, composed primarily of silicate rocks and metals. Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars are the solar system's terrestrial planets.

albedo.definition: The fraction of sunlight reflected by a surface, from 0 (absorbs all light) to 1 (reflects all light). Fresh snow has an albedo around 0.9; asphalt around 0.04.

au.definition: The average distance from the Earth to the Sun, approximately 149.6 million km (93 million miles). Used as a standard unit for measuring distances within the solar system.

light-year.definition: The distance light travels in one year in a vacuum: approximately 9.461 trillion km (5.879 trillion miles). Used to measure interstellar and intergalactic distances.

parsec.definition: A unit of distance equal to about 3.26 light-years (30.86 trillion km). Defined as the distance at which one AU subtends an angle of one arcsecond.

red dwarf.definition: A small, cool star with a surface temperature below 4,000 K and a mass less than 0.6 solar masses. The most common type of star in the Milky Way.

neutron star.definition: The ultra-dense collapsed core of a massive star after a supernova. A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 6 billion tonnes on Earth.

black hole.definition: A region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape once past the event horizon. Formed from collapsed massive stars or found at galaxy centers.

nebula.definition: A giant cloud of gas and dust in space. Some nebulae are regions where new stars are born; others are the remnants of dead or dying stars.

galaxy.definition: A gravitationally bound system of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter. The Milky Way contains 100-400 billion stars and is about 100,000 light-years across.

solar wind.definition: A continuous stream of charged particles (mostly protons and electrons) released from the Sun's corona at speeds of 300-800 km/s.

magnetosphere.definition: The region around a planet dominated by its magnetic field. Earth's magnetosphere deflects solar wind and traps charged particles in the Van Allen belts.

lagrange point.definition: One of five positions (L1-L5) in a two-body orbital system where a small object can maintain a stable position relative to both large bodies. L2 hosts the James Webb Space Telescope.

escape velocity.definition: The minimum speed an object needs to escape a celestial body's gravitational pull without further propulsion. Earth's escape velocity is about 11.2 km/s.

orbital period.definition: The time it takes for an object to complete one full orbit around another body. Earth's orbital period around the Sun is approximately 365.25 days.

synodic period.definition: The time between two successive conjunctions of a planet with the Sun as seen from Earth. It determines how often a planet appears in the same position in the sky.

sidereal period.definition: The time for a celestial object to complete one orbit relative to the fixed stars. Earth's sidereal day is about 23 hours 56 minutes.

retrograde.definition: Motion or rotation in the opposite direction to the primary body's rotation. Venus rotates retrograde, and planets appear to move retrograde when Earth overtakes them.

prograde.definition: Motion or rotation in the same direction as the primary body's rotation. Most planets orbit the Sun prograde (counterclockwise when viewed from above the north pole).

barycenter.definition: The common center of mass around which two or more bodies orbit. The Earth-Moon barycenter is about 4,670 km from Earth's center (inside Earth).

tidal locking.definition: When a body's orbital period matches its rotational period, causing the same face to always point toward the body it orbits. The Moon is tidally locked to Earth.

roche limit.definition: The minimum distance at which a celestial body held together only by gravity can orbit without being torn apart by tidal forces. Planetary rings form within this limit.

combustion engine.definition: A heat engine that converts chemical energy from fuel into mechanical work by burning the fuel inside cylinders. Internal combustion engines power most cars and trucks.

electric motor.definition: A device that converts electrical energy into mechanical motion using electromagnetic interactions. Efficiency typically ranges from 85-95%, far higher than combustion engines.

torque.definition: A rotational force that causes an object to spin. Measured in newton-meters (Nm) or foot-pounds (ft-lb). Higher torque means more twisting power.

horsepower.definition: A unit of power equal to approximately 746 watts. Originally defined by James Watt to compare steam engine output to draft horses.

rpm.definition: A measure of rotational speed indicating how many complete turns an object makes in one minute. Car engines typically idle at 600-1000 RPM.

gear ratio.definition: The ratio of teeth between two meshing gears, determining the trade-off between speed and torque. A 3:1 ratio triples torque but reduces speed by a third.

hydraulic.definition: A system that uses pressurized liquid (usually oil) to transmit force. Hydraulic systems can multiply force, powering heavy machinery like excavators and brakes.

pneumatic.definition: A system that uses compressed air or gas to transmit force and do work. Commonly used in tools like nail guns, jackhammers, and air brakes.

tensile strength.definition: The maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched before breaking. Measured in pascals (Pa) or PSI. Steel: ~400-550 MPa; spider silk: ~1,000 MPa.

yield strength.definition: The stress at which a material begins to deform permanently (plastically). Below yield strength, the material returns to its original shape when the load is removed.

thermal conductivity.definition: A measure of how well a material conducts heat, in watts per meter-kelvin (W/mK). Copper: 401, aluminum: 237, wood: 0.12, air: 0.024.

resistivity.definition: A material's intrinsic resistance to electric current flow, measured in ohm-meters. Lower resistivity means better conductivity. Copper: 1.68e-8; rubber: ~1e13.

capacitance.definition: The ability of a component to store electrical charge, measured in farads (F). Capacitors store and release energy quickly, smoothing voltage in circuits.

inductance.definition: The tendency of an electrical conductor to oppose changes in current flow by generating a magnetic field. Measured in henries (H). Used in transformers and filters.

voltage.definition: The electrical potential difference between two points, measured in volts (V). It is the 'pressure' that pushes electric charge through a circuit.

amperage.definition: The flow rate of electric charge through a conductor, measured in amperes (A). One amp equals one coulomb of charge passing a point per second.

wattage.definition: The rate of energy transfer or consumption, measured in watts (W). Watts = volts x amps. A typical household circuit handles 1,800-2,400 watts.

ohm.definition: The SI unit of electrical resistance. One ohm allows one ampere of current to flow when one volt of potential difference is applied. Ohm's law: V = IR.

transistor.definition: A semiconductor device used to amplify or switch electronic signals. The fundamental building block of modern electronics; a modern CPU contains billions.

semiconductor.definition: A material with electrical conductivity between a conductor and an insulator. Silicon is the most common. Doping with impurities controls conductivity for electronic devices.

alloy.definition: A mixture of metals (or a metal with other elements) designed to have specific properties. Examples: steel (iron+carbon), bronze (copper+tin), brass (copper+zinc).

tempering.definition: A heat treatment process that reduces the brittleness of hardened steel by reheating it to a temperature below critical point, then cooling. Balances hardness with toughness.

annealing.definition: A heat treatment process where a material is heated and slowly cooled to relieve internal stresses, increase ductility, and refine grain structure.

welding.definition: A fabrication process that joins materials by melting the workpieces together, often with a filler material. Common types: MIG, TIG, stick, and spot welding.

forging.definition: Shaping metal by applying compressive forces, usually with a hammer or press. Forging aligns the grain structure, producing parts stronger than cast equivalents.

casting.definition: A manufacturing process where molten material is poured into a mold and allowed to solidify. Used for complex shapes that would be difficult to machine.

hemp.definition: A variety of the Cannabis sativa plant grown for industrial use. Strong natural fiber used for textiles, rope, paper, and building materials. One of the oldest cultivated crops.

cotton.definition: A soft, fluffy natural fiber from the cotton plant's seed pods. The most widely used natural textile fiber, known for breathability and comfort.

polyester.definition: A synthetic polymer fiber made from petroleum-based chemicals. Durable, wrinkle-resistant, and quick-drying. The world's most produced synthetic fiber.

nylon.definition: A synthetic thermoplastic polymer. Strong, elastic, and resistant to abrasion. Used in clothing, rope, parachutes, and mechanical parts like gears and bearings.

kevlar.definition: An aramid fiber five times stronger than steel by weight. Used in body armor, tires, racing sails, and aerospace applications. Heat-resistant to 427 degrees C.

carbon fiber.definition: Extremely strong, lightweight fibers made of carbon atoms bonded in a crystalline structure. Tensile strength up to 7 GPa. Used in aerospace, sports equipment, and vehicles.

fiberglass.definition: A composite material made of glass fibers embedded in a polymer resin. Lightweight, strong, and corrosion-resistant. Used in boats, insulation, and car bodies.

steel.definition: An alloy of iron and carbon (0.2-2.1% carbon). The most widely used metal, with thousands of grades varying in strength, hardness, and corrosion resistance.

aluminum.definition: A lightweight, silvery metal with excellent corrosion resistance due to its oxide layer. Density is about one-third that of steel. Widely used in aerospace and packaging.

titanium.definition: A strong, lightweight, corrosion-resistant metal. As strong as steel but 45% lighter. Used in aerospace, medical implants, and high-performance applications.

copper.definition: A reddish-orange metal with excellent electrical and thermal conductivity. Used in wiring, plumbing, electronics, and as a base for alloys like brass and bronze.

brass.definition: An alloy of copper and zinc with a gold-like appearance. Corrosion-resistant, antimicrobial, and easy to machine. Used in fittings, instruments, and decorative items.

bronze.definition: An alloy primarily of copper and tin. Harder than pure copper, corrosion-resistant, and historically significant as the first widely used metal alloy (Bronze Age).

iron.definition: The most common element on Earth by mass. A silvery-gray metal that is the primary component of steel. Pure iron is relatively soft; alloying greatly increases strength.

nickel.definition: A hard, silvery-white metal resistant to corrosion. Used in stainless steel, batteries (NiMH, NiCd), coins, and high-temperature superalloys for jet engines.

cobalt.definition: A hard, lustrous, silver-blue metal. Critical for lithium-ion battery cathodes, superalloys, and blue pigments. Most production comes from the Democratic Republic of Congo.

tungsten.definition: The metal with the highest melting point (3,422 degrees C) and highest tensile strength at temperatures above 1,650 degrees C. Used in cutting tools, light bulb filaments, and armor.

platinum.definition: A dense, precious, silvery-white metal. Highly resistant to corrosion. Used in catalytic converters, jewelry, laboratory equipment, and cancer treatment drugs.

silicon.definition: A metalloid and the second most abundant element in Earth's crust. The foundation of modern electronics as the primary semiconductor material. Also used in glass and concrete.

ceramic.definition: An inorganic, nonmetallic solid prepared by heating and cooling. Extremely hard, heat-resistant, and electrically insulating. Used in tiles, pottery, and high-tech applications.

concrete.definition: A composite material of cement, water, and aggregates (sand, gravel). The most widely used construction material in the world. Strong in compression but weak in tension.

plywood.definition: An engineered wood made from thin layers (plies) of wood veneer glued together with alternating grain directions. This cross-graining reduces warping and increases strength.

hardwood.definition: Wood from angiosperm (flowering, broad-leaved) trees like oak, maple, walnut, and mahogany. Generally denser and more durable than softwoods, used for furniture and flooring.

softwood.definition: Wood from gymnosperm trees (conifers) like pine, spruce, and cedar. Grows faster than hardwood and is the primary material for construction lumber and paper pulp.

bamboo.definition: A fast-growing grass with a tensile strength comparable to steel. Used in construction, textiles, paper, and food. Some species grow up to 91 cm per day.

leather.definition: A durable material made by tanning animal hides, typically cattle. Flexible, breathable, and long-lasting. Used for clothing, footwear, upholstery, and accessories.

rubber.definition: An elastic polymer, either natural (from rubber tree latex) or synthetic (from petroleum). Excellent for seals, tires, hoses, and vibration dampening.

glass.definition: An amorphous solid typically made by rapidly cooling molten silica. Transparent, hard, and chemically inert. Used in windows, lenses, containers, and fiber optics.

polycarbonate.definition: A strong, transparent thermoplastic. Impact resistance is 250 times greater than glass. Used in eyewear, bulletproof windows, phone cases, and medical devices.

shelter.definition: A structure providing protection from weather, temperature extremes, and hazards. In survival, shelter is often the first priority after immediate safety.

potable water.definition: Water that is safe to drink, free of harmful pathogens and contaminants. An average person needs 2-3 liters per day in temperate conditions, more in heat or exertion.

filtration.definition: The process of removing particles and some pathogens from water by passing it through a porous material. Filters can use sand, ceramic, activated carbon, or membranes.

purification.definition: The process of removing or killing pathogens in water. Methods include boiling (1 minute at a rolling boil), chemical treatment (iodine, chlorine), and UV light exposure.

tinder.definition: Dry, easily combustible material used to start a fire. Examples: dry leaves, bark shavings, cotton balls, dryer lint. Must catch a spark and sustain a small flame.

kindling.definition: Small sticks and twigs used to build a fire after tinder ignites. Typically pencil-thickness or smaller. Bridges the gap between tinder flame and larger fuel wood.

hypothermia.definition: A dangerous drop in core body temperature below 35 degrees C (95 degrees F). Symptoms progress from shivering to confusion, drowsiness, and cardiac arrest if untreated.

dehydration.definition: A deficit of total body water caused by insufficient intake or excess loss. Losing just 2% body weight in water impairs cognitive and physical performance.

calorie.definition: A unit of energy. In nutrition, 'calorie' usually means kilocalorie (kcal). Average adults need 1,800-2,500 kcal per day; strenuous activity can require 3,000-5,000+ kcal.

protein.definition: A macronutrient made of amino acids, essential for building and repairing tissue. Recommended intake is 0.8 g per kg body weight daily. Sources: meat, fish, legumes, dairy.

carbohydrate.definition: A macronutrient that provides the body's primary energy source. Includes sugars, starches, and fiber. Found in grains, fruits, vegetables, and legumes.

fat.definition: A macronutrient providing 9 kcal per gram (more than twice protein or carbs). Essential for hormone production, nutrient absorption, and insulation. Includes saturated and unsaturated types.

vitamin.definition: An organic compound required in small quantities for normal metabolism. 13 essential vitamins: A, C, D, E, K, and 8 B vitamins. Deficiencies cause specific diseases.

mineral.definition: Inorganic elements essential for body functions. Major minerals: calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, sodium, potassium, chloride. Trace minerals: iron, zinc, copper, selenium.

first aid.definition: Initial emergency care given to an injured or ill person before professional medical treatment. Priorities: airway, breathing, circulation (ABCs), then bleeding control.

tourniquet.definition: A device used to stop severe bleeding in a limb by compressing blood vessels. Applied 2-3 inches above the wound. Modern combat tourniquets save lives in minutes.

splint.definition: A rigid or semi-rigid device used to immobilize a fractured or injured limb. Can be improvised from sticks, boards, or rolled-up magazines padded with cloth.

suture.definition: A stitch or series of stitches used to close a wound. Materials include absorbable (gut, polyglycolic acid) and non-absorbable (nylon, silk) threads.

antiseptic.definition: A substance applied to living tissue to reduce infection by killing or inhibiting microorganisms. Examples: iodine, hydrogen peroxide, alcohol, chlorhexidine.

antibiotics.definition: Medications that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria. Only effective against bacterial infections, not viruses. Overuse leads to antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

blueprint.definition: A detailed technical drawing or plan for construction or manufacturing. In gaming contexts, a template defining the structure, components, and assembly of a buildable object.

foundation.definition: The structural base that transfers a building's load to the ground. Types include slab, crawl space, basement, pier, and pile foundations depending on soil and conditions.

load-bearing.definition: A structural element that carries and transfers weight from above to the foundation. Load-bearing walls cannot be removed without alternative support or the structure may collapse.

stud.definition: A vertical framing member in a wall, typically 2x4 or 2x6 lumber spaced 16 or 24 inches apart. Studs form the skeleton of wood-framed walls.

joist.definition: A horizontal structural member that spans an open space, typically between beams, to support a floor or ceiling. Common sizes: 2x8, 2x10, or 2x12 lumber.

rafter.definition: A sloped structural member that extends from the ridge of a roof to the eaves, supporting the roof deck and shingles. Cut individually and installed on-site.

truss.definition: A prefabricated triangulated framework that spans long distances with high strength-to-weight ratio. Used in roofs, bridges, and towers. Triangles resist deformation.

drywall.definition: A panel of gypsum plaster pressed between two thick sheets of paper, used to make interior walls and ceilings. Standard thickness: 1/2 inch. Also called sheetrock or plasterboard.

insulation.definition: Material that reduces heat transfer between the interior and exterior of a building. Types: fiberglass batts, spray foam, cellulose, rigid foam board, mineral wool.

r-value.definition: A measure of thermal resistance in insulation. Higher R-value means better insulating ability. Recommended values vary by climate zone: R-13 to R-60 for attics in cold climates.

btu.definition: A unit of heat energy. One BTU raises one pound of water by 1 degree F. Used to rate heating/cooling capacity: a typical room AC unit is 5,000-12,000 BTU.

hvac.definition: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning. The technology of indoor climate control. Systems regulate temperature, humidity, and air quality in buildings.

plumbing.definition: The system of pipes, fixtures, and fittings for water supply and waste removal in a building. Materials include copper, PVC, PEX, and galvanized steel.

wiring.definition: The system of electrical conductors that distribute power throughout a building. Residential wiring typically uses copper wire in NM (Romex) cable rated for 15 or 20 amps.

circuit breaker.definition: A safety device that automatically interrupts electrical current when it exceeds a safe level, preventing wire overheating and fires. Resettable alternative to fuses.

grounding.definition: Connecting an electrical system to the earth to provide a safe path for fault currents. Prevents electric shock and equipment damage. The green or bare wire in circuits.

solder.definition: A fusible metal alloy used to join metal workpieces by melting between them. Electronics solder is typically tin-lead or lead-free tin-silver-copper alloy.

rivet.definition: A permanent mechanical fastener with a head on one end. Installed by deforming the tail end to clamp pieces together. Used in bridges, aircraft, and structural steel.

bolt.definition: A threaded fastener used with a nut to clamp materials together. Unlike screws, bolts are tightened by turning the nut. Grades indicate tensile strength (Grade 5, 8, etc.).

nut.definition: A threaded metal piece that mates with a bolt to secure joints. Types include hex, lock (nylon insert), wing, castle, and flanged nuts.

washer.definition: A thin disc placed under a nut or bolt head to distribute load, prevent loosening, or protect the surface. Types: flat, lock (split), spring, and fender washers.

gasket.definition: A compressible seal placed between two surfaces to prevent leaks of fluids or gases under pressure. Materials: rubber, cork, paper, metal, or composite.

bearing.definition: A machine element that constrains relative motion to the desired direction and reduces friction. Types: ball, roller, plain (sleeve), and thrust bearings.

bushing.definition: A cylindrical lining that reduces friction and wear between rotating shafts and stationary supports. Similar to a plain bearing but typically a separate removable component.

cam.definition: A rotating or sliding piece in a mechanism that converts rotary motion to linear motion. Used in engines (camshafts open/close valves) and many industrial machines.

crankshaft.definition: A shaft with offset cranks that converts the linear (reciprocating) motion of pistons into rotational motion. The backbone of internal combustion engines.

piston.definition: A cylindrical component that moves up and down inside an engine cylinder. Connected to the crankshaft via a connecting rod. Compresses fuel-air mixture and transmits combustion force.

ecs.definition: An architectural pattern for game engines where entities are IDs, components are data, and systems are logic. Enables cache-friendly iteration and flexible composition over inheritance.

component.definition: A plain data struct attached to an entity in an ECS architecture. Contains no logic. Examples: Position, Velocity, Health, Sprite. Multiple components compose an entity's behavior.

system.definition: A function or object that operates on entities with specific component combinations. Systems contain the game logic: physics, rendering, AI, input handling.

entity.definition: A unique identifier (usually an integer) in an ECS that represents a game object. Has no data or behavior of its own; identity comes from attached components.

tick.definition: A single update cycle of the game loop. Each tick, systems process entities, physics advance, and the world state updates. Server tick rate determines simulation frequency.

delta time.definition: The elapsed time between the current and previous frame (dt). Used to make movement frame-rate-independent: position += velocity * dt. Typically measured in seconds.

interpolation.definition: Estimating values between two known data points. In games, used to smooth movement between physics ticks or network updates. Linear interpolation (lerp) is the simplest form.

dead reckoning.definition: Predicting an entity's future position using its last known position, velocity, and acceleration. Used in networked games to reduce perceived latency between server updates.

lod.definition: A technique that reduces the complexity of 3D models based on distance from the camera. Distant objects use fewer polygons, improving rendering performance without visible quality loss.

frustum culling.definition: An optimization that skips rendering objects outside the camera's view pyramid (frustum). Typically eliminates 50-90% of scene objects from the rendering pipeline.

octree.definition: A tree data structure where each node has exactly eight children, used to partition 3D space. Efficient for spatial queries, collision detection, and sparse voxel storage.

voxel.definition: A 3D pixel representing a value on a regular grid in three-dimensional space. Used in games like Minecraft for destructible terrain and in medical imaging (CT/MRI scans).

icosphere.definition: A sphere mesh created by subdividing an icosahedron (20-faced polyhedron). Produces more uniform triangle sizes than UV spheres, making it ideal for planet rendering with LOD.

mesh.definition: A collection of vertices, edges, and faces that define the shape of a 3D object. Triangle meshes are the most common format used by GPUs for real-time rendering.

vertex.definition: A point in 3D space defined by its position (x, y, z). Vertices also carry attributes like normal vectors, UV coordinates, and vertex colors used during rendering.

normal.definition: A unit vector perpendicular to a surface at a given point. Used to calculate how light interacts with a surface. Smooth normals are interpolated across faces for curved appearance.

uv.definition: A 2D coordinate system used to map textures onto 3D surfaces. U and V represent horizontal and vertical axes of the texture. UV unwrapping is the process of creating this mapping.

shader.definition: A program that runs on the GPU to determine how pixels and vertices are rendered. Vertex shaders transform geometry; fragment (pixel) shaders determine final color.

render pipeline.definition: The sequence of steps the GPU takes to transform 3D scene data into a 2D image: vertex processing, rasterization, fragment processing, and output merging.

draw call.definition: A command from the CPU to the GPU to render a set of geometry. Each draw call has overhead, so minimizing their count through batching and instancing is key to performance.

instancing.definition: A GPU technique that renders multiple copies of the same mesh with a single draw call, each with different transforms. Ideal for trees, grass, particles, and repeated objects.

batch rendering.definition: Combining multiple objects that share the same material/shader into a single draw call. Reduces CPU-GPU communication overhead and dramatically improves frame rates.

ed25519.definition: A public-key digital signature algorithm using Curve25519. Produces 64-byte signatures with 32-byte keys. Fast, secure, and deterministic. Used in SSH, TLS, and blockchain systems.

public key.definition: The shareable half of a cryptographic key pair. Used to verify signatures and encrypt messages. In HumanityOS, your public key is your identity and wallet address.

private key.definition: The secret half of a cryptographic key pair. Used to create signatures and decrypt messages. Must never be shared. Loss means permanent loss of access to the identity.

seed phrase.definition: A human-readable backup of a cryptographic key, typically 12 or 24 English words generated from a random number. Can regenerate the full key pair. Must be stored securely offline.

bip39.definition: Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 39, a standard for generating mnemonic seed phrases from entropy. Uses a wordlist of 2,048 English words. Adopted widely beyond Bitcoin.

self-custody.definition: Holding your own cryptographic keys rather than entrusting them to a third party. Gives full control over assets and identity but requires personal responsibility for key security.

custodial.definition: A service where a third party holds and manages your cryptographic keys on your behalf (like a bank holding your money). Convenient but introduces counterparty risk.

noncustodial.definition: A wallet or service where only you hold the private keys. No third party can access, freeze, or move your funds. Requires safeguarding your own seed phrase or key file.

solana.definition: A high-performance blockchain supporting up to 65,000 transactions per second with ~400ms block times. Uses Proof of Stake with Proof of History for ordering. HumanityOS's chosen chain.

spl token.definition: Solana Program Library token standard, equivalent to Ethereum's ERC-20. Defines how fungible and non-fungible tokens are created and transferred on the Solana blockchain.

staking.definition: Locking cryptocurrency in a network to support blockchain operations (validation, security). Stakers earn rewards proportional to their stake. Helps secure Proof of Stake networks.

validator.definition: A node that participates in blockchain consensus by verifying transactions and proposing new blocks. Validators stake tokens as collateral and earn rewards for honest behavior.

transaction.definition: A signed instruction submitted to the blockchain to transfer tokens, execute a smart contract, or modify on-chain state. Each transaction has a unique signature for verification.

signature.definition: A cryptographic proof that a message was created by the holder of a specific private key. Anyone with the public key can verify it, but cannot forge it.

hash.definition: A fixed-size output produced by a hash function from arbitrary input. Even a tiny change in input produces a completely different hash. Used for data integrity and passwords.

blake3.definition: A cryptographic hash function that is significantly faster than SHA-256 while maintaining security. Supports parallel processing. Used in many modern systems for hashing and key derivation.

cbor.definition: Concise Binary Object Representation: a binary data serialization format similar to JSON but smaller and faster to parse. Used in IoT, WebAuthn, and COSE (cryptographic message format).
